original_hypothesis.title: Changing transferAllowedContract Away From MergeTgt Permanently Locks All TITN Claims
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: MergeTgt is deployed as the transferAllowedContract on Arbitrum (per arbitrumSetup.ts). On Arbitrum (chainId 42161), ALL transfers are blocked except for exempted addresses. MergeTgt relies on being the transferAllowedContract to transfer TITN to users in claimTitn() and withdrawRemainingTitn(). If the owner calls setTransferAllowedContract() to change it to a different address (e.g., a staking contract or DEX), MergeTgt loses its exemption. All safeTransfer calls in MergeTgt would revert due to transfer restrictions, permanently locking user claims. Additionally, MergeTgt is marked as isBridgedTokenHolder (TITN was bridged to it via sendToArb.ts), making the restriction apply on any chain.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a tight coupling between MergeTgt's claim functionality and Titn's transfer restriction system. There is only ONE transferAllowedContract slot. Changing it is a plausible admin action (e.g., to allow a staking contract or DEX). The consequence — permanently bricking all MergeTgt claims — is non-obvious and severe. The only recovery is calling setBridgedTokenTransferLocked(false), which disables ALL transfer restrictions globally.
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: arbitrumSetup.ts line: await titn.setTransferAllowedContract(deploymentMergeTgt.address) confirms MergeTgt is set as transferAllowedContract. sendToArb.ts bridges TITN to MergeTgt address, marking it as isBridgedTokenHolder.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Single transferAllowedContract slot. MergeTgt relies on being this address to transfer TITN. On Arbitrum (chainId 42161), block.chainid == arbitrumChainId is always true, so ALL non-exempt transfers revert. If transferAllowedContract changes, MergeTgt's from-address is no longer exempt.

preconditions: Owner calls setTransferAllowedContract to a different address
Users have unclaimed TITN in MergeTgt

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Only ONE transferAllowedContract slot; MergeTgt is tightly coupled to this being set to its own address
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Admin changes transferAllowedContract → MergeTgt safeTransfer reverts → all claims locked
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No attacker profit — all user claims permanently locked. Admin must disable all transfer restrictions to recover.

economic_questions: Can setBridgedTokenTransferLocked(false) fully recover claim functionality?
What is the impact of globally disabling transfer restrictions as a fix?
Is there any plan to change transferAllowedContract during the 360-day period?
validation_requirements: Test claimTitn after changing transferAllowedContract away from MergeTgt
Verify MergeTgt is marked as isBridgedTokenHolder after TITN bridge
Confirm the only recovery is setBridgedTokenTransferLocked(false)

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: N/A — admin action triggers the issue
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: No direct profit — DoS on all claims

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All MergeTgt users with unclaimed TITN

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Simulate transferAllowedContract change and verify claimTitn reverts

validation_result.mechanism: single-slot transfer exemption creates destructive coupling
validation_result.impact_summary: Changing transferAllowedContract away from MergeTgt permanently blocks all TITN claims (claimTitn and withdrawRemainingTitn), locking up to 173.7M TITN. The only recovery is globally disabling all transfer restrictions.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: MergeTgt relies on being the transferAllowedContract in Titn.sol to transfer TITN tokens to users during claimTitn() and withdrawRemainingTitn(). On Arbitrum (chainId 42161), ALL non-exempt transfers revert due to the condition `block.chainid == arbitrumChainId` in _validateTransfer(). There is only one transferAllowedContract slot. If the owner calls setTransferAllowedContract() to a different address (e.g., a DEX or staking contract), MergeTgt loses its exemption. Every subsequent call to claimTitn() or withdrawRemainingTitn() will revert at the titn.safeTransfer() call because _validateTransfer will reject the transfer from MergeTgt (non-owner, non-exempt, on Arbitrum chain). This is a genuine code defect: the single-slot design creates a destructive coupling where a plausible admin action permanently breaks core user-facing functionality.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The evidence is unambiguous. Titn._validateTransfer (lines 72-84) checks `from != transferAllowedContract && to != transferAllowedContract` as part of a conjunctive condition. On Arbitrum, `block.chainid == arbitrumChainId` is always true, making ALL non-exempt transfers revert. MergeTgt.claimTitn (line 104) and withdrawRemainingTitn (line 131) both call titn.safeTransfer(), which triggers transfer() → _validateTransfer(). If MergeTgt is not the transferAllowedContract, the from address (MergeTgt) is not exempt, and the transfer reverts.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Only one transferAllowedContract slot exists. On Arbitrum (chainId 42161), the block.chainid check makes ALL non-exempt transfers revert. If this slot is changed away from MergeTgt, MergeTgt's transfers are no longer exempt, breaking all claim functionality.
Single-slot setter that overwrites the previous value. No check that MergeTgt (or any critical contract) needs to remain exempt. No support for multiple exempt addresses.

titn.safeTransfer(msg.sender, amount) at line 104 calls Titn.transfer() which invokes _validateTransfer(MergeTgt_address, user). If MergeTgt is not the transferAllowedContract, this reverts on Arbitrum.
Same issue as claimTitn - the safeTransfer call will revert if MergeTgt is no longer the transferAllowedContract.

calling_sequence: 2. MergeTgt calls titn.safeTransfer(msg.sender, amount) at line 104
6. from != owner() → true (MergeTgt is not Titn owner)
7. from != transferAllowedContract → true (if changed away from MergeTgt)
8. to != transferAllowedContract → true (user != new allowed contract)
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: MergeTgt.claimTitn → titn.safeTransfer → Titn.transfer → _validateTransfer. On Arbitrum, the block.chainid == 42161 check universally triggers the restriction path. If MergeTgt is not the transferAllowedContract (either as from or to), the transfer reverts. The same path applies to withdrawRemainingTitn at line 131.

deployment_assumptions_checked: MergeTgt is set as transferAllowedContract in arbitrumSetup.ts
TITN is bridged to MergeTgt via sendToArb.ts, marking it as isBridgedTokenHolder
isBridgedTokensTransferLocked defaults to true in constructor
config_validation.deployment_evidence: arbitrumSetup.ts line: `await titn.setTransferAllowedContract(deploymentMergeTgt.address)` confirms MergeTgt is initially the transferAllowedContract. sendToArb.ts bridges 173.7M TITN to MergeTgt address (0x5166ef11e5dF6D4Ca213778fFf4756937e469663), which triggers _credit and sets isBridgedTokenHolder[MergeTgt] = true. Titn constructor sets isBridgedTokensTransferLocked = true.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: This is not an attack but a dangerous admin misconfiguration path. The owner may legitimately want to change transferAllowedContract to enable a DEX or staking contract to trade/stake TITN. Doing so immediately and permanently blocks all MergeTgt claims. The consequence is non-obvious because: (1) the single-slot design doesn't communicate the dependency, (2) there's no warning in the code or comments, and (3) the MergeTgt contract has no reference to the transferAllowedContract concept. Recovery requires calling setBridgedTokenTransferLocked(false) which globally disables ALL transfer restrictions, not just for MergeTgt.
validation_result.impact_assessment: All unclaimed TITN (up to 173.7M TITN) would be permanently locked in MergeTgt. Every user with claimableTitnPerUser > 0 would be unable to claim. The only recovery path is setBridgedTokenTransferLocked(false), which removes all transfer restrictions globally - a heavy-handed fix that may compromise other security invariants. Alternatively, the owner could set transferAllowedContract back to MergeTgt, but this would break whatever new contract needed the exemption.
recommended_mitigations: Replace single transferAllowedContract with a mapping(address => bool) of allowed contracts, supporting multiple exemptions simultaneously
Add a check in setTransferAllowedContract that warns or prevents removing MergeTgt while it holds TITN balance
Add an immutable reference to MergeTgt in Titn.sol that is always exempt from transfer restrictions
Consider using a whitelist pattern: mapping(address => bool) public isTransferAllowed, with addTransferAllowed/removeTransferAllowed functions
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy Titn and MergeTgt on a fork of Arbitrum (chainId 42161). 2. Run arbitrumSetup.ts to set MergeTgt as transferAllowedContract and deposit TITN. 3. Have a user deposit TGT via transferAndCall to get claimableTitnPerUser > 0. 4. Verify claimTitn works. 5. Call setTransferAllowedContract(someOtherAddress). 6. Attempt claimTitn → expect revert with BridgedTokensTransferLocked. 7. Attempt withdrawRemainingTitn (after 360 days) → expect same revert.
uncertainties: Whether the project team has operational procedures preventing this admin action
Whether a timelock or multisig on the owner account would mitigate the risk in practice
additional_testing_needed: Test claimTitn after changing transferAllowedContract to verify the revert
Test setBridgedTokenTransferLocked(false) as recovery and verify it fully restores claim functionality
Test whether any other recovery path exists beyond the two identified options
